concepts.title: Displacement and distance
concepts.conceptBody:
Introduction to Rest and Motion

  1. Particle: A particle is a point mass. However, in practice a body may be treated as a particle, if its size is very small compared to the distance covered by it.
  2. Position: The position of a particle refers to its location in the space at a certain moment of time. In general, the position is measured by a vector joining a fixed point (known as origin) to the moving particle. This vector is known as position vector.

For a particle in straight line motion along $\text{X}$-axis, the position vector is always parallel to $\text{X}$-axis and hence has only X-component as non-zero. Therefore, the position of a moving particle can be measured by the $\text{X}$-coordinate $\text{x}\left( \text{t} \right)$ at a certain time instant $\text{t}$.

If a particle is moving in a curve (i.e., in a plane) the position vector can have many possible directions. The position in such a case can be measured by two numbers: X-coordinate and Y-coordinate the position vector.

  1. Distance is a scalar quantity which refers to "how much ground an object has covered" during its motion. It is the actual length of the path travelled by a particle.
  2. Displacement is a vector quantity which refers to "how far out of place an object is". It is the vector joining the initial position of the particle to its final position during an interval of time. The change in the position of a moving object is known as displacement

(a) To test your understanding of this distinction, consider the motion depicted in the diagram below. A physics teacher walks 4 meters East, 2 meters South, 4 meters West, and finally 2 meters North.

Even though the physics teacher has walked a total distance of 12 meters, her displacement is 0 meters. During the course of her motion, she has "covered 12 meters of ground" (distance $=12\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$). Yet, when she is finished walking, she is not "out of place" - i.e., there is no displacement for her motion (displacement$=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}$). Displacement, being a vector quantity, must give attention to direction. The 4 meters east is canceled by the 4 meters west; and the 2 meters south is canceled by the 2 meters north.

(b) If a particle goes from $\text{A}$ to $\text{B}$ along a curve in some time duration and if $\text{O}$ is the origin then

$\overline{\text{OA}}=\text{ initial }\text{ position }\text{ vector }={{\bar{r}}_{\text{i}}}$

$\overline{\text{OB}}=\text{ final }\text{ position }\text{ vector }={{\bar{r}}_{\text{f}}}$

$\text{AB}=\text{ displacement }\text{ vector }=\overline{\text{OB}}-\overline{\text{OA}}$

$\Rightarrow \bar{s}={{\bar{r}}_{\text{f}}}-{{\bar{r}}_{\text{i}}}=\Delta\bar{r}$

concepts.title: Speed and Velocity
concepts.conceptBody:
Speed and Velocity

  • Speed: Speed is a scalar quantity which refers to "how fast an object is moving. "Rate of change of distance $\left( \text{x} \right)$ covered by a particle, with time is called the speed of the particle. Its unit is $\text{m}/\text{s}$.
  • Velocity: Velocity is a vector quantity which refers to "the rate at which an object changes its position." Rate of change of displacement of the particle with time is called velocity of the particle.

concepts.title: Average speed
concepts.conceptBody:
Average speed

The average speed of a particle in a time interval is defined as the distance travelled by the particle by the time interval. If the particle travels a distance $s$ in time ${{t}_{1}}$ to ${{t}_{2}}$ the average speed is defined as:

$\text{ speed}{{}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{\text{s}}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}}}$

concepts.title: Average Velocity
concepts.conceptBody:
Average velocity

The average velocity of a particle in a time interval ${{t}_{1}}$ to ${{t}_{2}}$ is defined as its displacement divided by the time interval.

\({{\vec{v}}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{{\vec{s}}}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}}}\left( \vec{s}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ displacement}\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}\tilde{i}+\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ y}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}\tilde{j}\)

concepts.title: Instantaneous Speed
concepts.conceptBody:
Instantaneous speed

The instantaneous speed is the speed of the particle defined for a particular instant. When we had described average speed, it meant speed of the particle defined for a time interval.

Suppose a particle covered a distance $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ s}$ for a time interval $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$, then average speed in this case is equal to $\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ s}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}$

Now, if the interval $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$ is made extremely small, approaching to zero, the speed that is defined become for an instant. So, we can write the instantaneous speed as $\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}\to 0}}\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ s}}{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}}=\frac{\text{ds}}{\text{dt}}$. In general, when we talk of 'speed', it refers to the instantaneous speed.

You might think of the instantaneous speed as the speed which the speedometer reads at any given instant in time and the average speed as the average of all the speedometer readings during the course of the trip.

concepts.title: Acceleration
concepts.conceptBody:
Acceleration

Rate of change of velocity with time is called acceleration. Acceleration is a vector quantity. Its unit is $\text{m}/{{\text{s}}^{2}}$.

Direction of the Acceleration Vector 

Acceleration is a vector quantity so it will always have a direction associated with it. The direction of the acceleration vector depends on two factors:

  • whether the object is speeding up or slowing down
  • whether the object is moving in the positive (+) or negative (-) direction

The general rule of thumb is if an object is slowing down, then its acceleration is in the opposite direction of its motion. This rule of thumb can be applied to determine whether the sign of the acceleration of an object is positive or negative, right or left, up or down, etc.

Average acceleration: Average acceleration is defined as the change in velocity divided by the time interval.

Average acceleration $=\frac{\text{ change }\text{ in }\text{ velocity }}{\text{ time }\text{ interval }}$

Thus, ${{\vec{a}}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{{{{\vec{v}}}_{2}}-{{{\vec{v}}}_{1}}}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}}}$

The average acceleration depends only on the acceleration at time ${{t}_{1}}$ and ${{t}_{2}}$. The way the velocity changed in between these times does not make a difference in defining average acceleration.

Instantaneous acceleration: The acceleration at a given instant of time is the called the instantaneous acceleration. When we refer to acceleration, we mean 'instantaneous acceleration', i.e.,

$\vec{a}=\underset{\Delta\text{ t}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{\Delta\vec{v}}{\Delta\text{ t}}=\frac{\Delta\vec{v}}{\text{dt}}$

We also know that $\vec{F}=\text{ma}$, which means that acceleration is also decided by the net force acting on a particle. If $\text{F}=0$, then $\text{a}=0$ and the motion is known as uniform motion because the velocity does not change, which also means that the particle is moving with a constant velocity. While, if the force is constant, acceleration is also constant and the motion is known as uniformly accelerated motion, which means that acceleration acts on the particle, but it is constant. An example of such a case is the free fall motion (force and acceleration do not change).

concepts.title: Instantaneous Velocity and Acceleration
concepts.conceptBody:
Instantaneous Velocity & Acceleration

Instantaneous Velocity

If a car is driven along a straight road for $10\text{ km}$ in 5 hours, the average velocity is $2\text{ km}/\text{hr}$. Can we say that the car was moving with $2\text{ km}/\text{hr}$ at every instant in these five hours? Certainly not. As we can easily imagine, the car accelerated from rest and went through will all speeds like $0.5\text{ km}/\text{hr}$, $1\text{ km}/\text{hr}$ etc (and it may be moving with speeds more than $2\text{ km}/\text{hr}$ at some instants). To deal with this, we need the concept of instantaneous velocity, which is the velocity at any instant of time.

Consider a particle moving along X-axis. Let us find the instantaneous velocity of this particle at a certain time instant $t$, when it passes through the point $\text{P}\left( \text{t} \right)$ as shown. We start with the concept of average velocity. The particle passes through the point $\text{Q}\left( \text{t}+\Delta\text{ t} \right)\Delta\text{ t}$ seconds after it passes $\text{P}\left( \text{t} \right)$. The ratio $\Delta\text{ X}/\Delta\text{ t}$ is the average velocity over the interval $\text{PQ}$.

${{\text{V}}_{\text{av}}}=\frac{\Delta\text{ x}}{\Delta\text{ t}}\left( \text{in }\text{ PQ} \right)$

By taking the position $\text{Q}\left( \text{t}+\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t} \right)$ more and more close to $\text{P}\left( \text{t} \right)$, we can make this average velocity very closely approximate the exact instantaneous velocity at $\text{P}$.

Taking Q closer to $\text{P}$ means that the interval $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }$ t shrinks and diminishes towards zero. We usually describe this situation like this: "as $\text{Q}$ is taken closer to $\text{P},\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ t}$ approaches zero and the average velocity in $\text{PQ}$ approaches the instantaneous velocity at $\text{P}$ ".

As $\text{Q}\to \text{P},\Delta\text{ t}=0$ and ${{\text{V}}_{\text{av}}}($ in $\text{PQ})\to {{\text{V}}_{\text{inst }}}($ at $\text{P})$

${{\text{V}}_{\text{inst }}}={{\left( \frac{\Delta\text{ x}}{\Delta\text{ t}}\text{ in }\text{ PQ} \right)}_{\text{Q}\to \text{P}}}={{\left( \frac{\Delta\text{ x}}{\Delta\text{ t}} \right)}_{\Delta\text{ t}\to 0}}$

In exact notation we write:

${{\text{V}}_{\text{inst }}}=\underset{\Delta\text{ t}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\left( \frac{\Delta\text{ x}}{\Delta\text{ t}} \right)=\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}$

In words, the instantaneous velocity at a given moment (say, $t$) is the limiting value of the average velocity as we let $\Delta\text{ t}$ approach zero. The limit as $\Delta\text{ t}\to 0$ is written in calculus notation as $\text{dx}/\text{dt}$ and is called the derivative of $\text{x}$ with respect to $\text{t}$.

It is important to note that we do not simply set $\Delta\text{ t}=0$, for then $\Delta\text{ x}$ would also be zero, and we would have an undefined number. We must consider the ratio $\Delta\text{ x}/\Delta\text{ t}$ as a whole; and let $\Delta\text{ t}$ approach zero, $\Delta\text{ x}$ approaches zero also; but the ratio $\Delta\text{ x}/\Delta\text{ t}$ approaches some definite value, which we call the instantaneous velocity.

NOTE: The determination of instantaneous velocity by using the definition (i.e. by the limiting process as in the last example) usually involves calculation. We can find

$\text{v}=\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}$ by using the standard results from differential calculus.

$\text{x}=\text{u}+\text{v}+\ldots \Rightarrow \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=\frac{\text{du}}{\text{dt}}+\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}}+\ldots $

$\text{x}=\text{constant }\Rightarrow \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=0$ (derivative of a constant is zero)

$\text{x}=\text{Au }\Rightarrow \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=\text{A}\frac{\text{du}}{\text{dt}}$ (where $\text{A}$ is a constant)

$\text{x}={{\text{t}}^{\text{n}}}\Rightarrow \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=\text{nt}{{\text{t}}^{\text{n}-1}}$

$x=\text{sin}\omega t\Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}=\omega \text{cos}\omega t(\omega$ is constant$)$

$x=\text{cos}\omega t\Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}=-\omega \text{sin}\omega t(\omega$ is constant$)$

$\text{xlogt }\Rightarrow \frac{\text{dx}}{\text{dt}}=\frac{1}{\text{t}}$

Instantaneous Acceleration

The instantaneous acceleration of a particle is its acceleration at a particular instant of time. It is defined as the derivative (rate of change) of velocity with respect to time:

$\bar{a}=\frac{\text{d}\bar{v}}{\text{dt}}=\underset{\Delta\text{ t}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\left( \frac{\Delta\bar{v}}{\Delta\text{ t}} \right).$

concepts.title: Acceleration in One Dimension
concepts.conceptBody:
Acceleration in One Dimension

Following points are worth noting in case of one dimensional motion with constant acceleration.

(i) It can be observed when either $\text{u}=0,\vec{u}\uparrow \uparrow \vec{a}$ or $\vec{u}\uparrow \downarrow \vec{a}$

(ii) In the first two cases when either $\text{u}=0$ or $\vec{u}\uparrow \uparrow \vec{a}$ motion is only accelerated.

(iii) When $\vec{u}\uparrow \downarrow \vec{a}$ a motion is first retarded (till the velocity becomes zero) and then accelerated in opposite direction.

(iv) As per our convention (vertically upward positive) acceleration due to gravity ' $\text{g}$ ' is always negative whether the particle is moving upwards or downwards. We are now left with the sign of $u$ and s. Displacement $\text{s}$ is measured from the point of projection.

(v) For fast calculation in objective problems, remember the following results:

(a) Maximum height attained by a particle, thrown upwards from ground

$\text{h}=\frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}}{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}$

(b) Velocity of particle at the time of striking the ground when released $\left( u=0 \right)$ from a height his,

$\text{v}=\sqrt{2\text{gh}}$

(c) In (b) time of collision with ground $\text{t}=\sqrt{\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}}$

(d) Displacement of particle in nth second of its motion,

${{\text{s}}_{1}}=\text{u}+\text{at}-\frac{1}{2}\text{a}$

(vi) Difference between distance (d) and displacement (s)

The 's' in equations of motion $\left( s=ut+\frac{1}{2}a{{t}^{2}} \right.$ and $\left. {{v}^{2}}={{u}^{2}}+2as \right)$ is really the displacement not the distance. They have different values only when $\text{u}$ and a are are of opposite sign or u $\uparrow \downarrow $ a

SOME COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS!!

MISCONCEPT 1 - Equations of motion are applicable every time.

CLARIFICATION - Equations of motion are applicable only when a is constant.

MISCONCEPT 2 - In eqn of motion, s gives distance

CLARIFICATION - s gives displacement, not distance. So for finding distance, we must find out instant at which velocity is reversing and then break the motion in parts.
